paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1128 English Language — AI-authored Simplified Practice 28: Newcomer (§B style, simplified band)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a transfer student's first morning at a new Singapore secondary school, when she gets lost and nervous and is quietly helped by a classmate, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [10 marks] — AI-authored simplified practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1128/02 Section B format, pitched at students stretching from middle secondary toward the full O-Level standard. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. 7 short-answer items (Q1–Q7) and a 4-item flowchart MCQ (Q8) are included.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
My family had moved to Singapore from Ipoh only two weeks before, and on the first morning of Secondary Three I stood at the gate of Greenview Secondary, gripping the strap of my new bag with both hands. The school was much bigger than my old one. Boys and girls in unfamiliar uniforms streamed past me, calling out jokes I did not understand. Nobody looked at me. I felt like a small grey stone dropped into a fast-moving river.

Paragraph 2
I had a printed timetable in my pocket: Form Class 3E, Block C, Level 4. That sounded simple enough. But the school was a maze of staircases and covered walkways, and every corridor looked the same. I climbed one staircase, then another, and somehow ended up at a locked science laboratory that smelled of chemicals. The bell rang, loud and final. My heart began to thump. I was already late, and I did not even know which way to turn.

Paragraph 3
I walked faster, then slower, then stopped altogether. A teacher hurried past with a stack of files and did not see me. I opened my mouth to ask, but no sound came out. Back home I had been one of the loudest girls in my class. Here my voice seemed to have shrunk to nothing. I blinked hard, telling myself I would not cry on the first day.

Paragraph 4
That was when I noticed a boy a few steps away, tying his shoelace by the railing. He had seen everything — my wrong turns, my panic, my shining eyes. He did not laugh. He straightened up, glanced at the timetable crumpled in my hand, and said quietly, 'Three E? Same class. Block C is the other building. Come, I'll show you.'

Paragraph 5
He did not make a big thing of it. He simply walked, and I followed, half a step behind. As we crossed the walkway he pointed out small landmarks — the canteen with the blue roof, the staircase near the big rain tree. 'Everyone gets lost,' he said, 'until they learn the rain tree.' I managed a small laugh, my first that morning.

Paragraph 6
We reached the classroom together, before the teacher had started taking attendance. The boy slipped into a seat near the back as if nothing had happened, and pointed me toward an empty desk by the window. 'You can sit there,' he whispered. I sat down, and for the first time since the move, the tight knot in my chest began to loosen.

Paragraph 7
At recess I expected him to disappear with his own friends, and he almost did. But before he went, he turned back and said, 'I'm Wei Han. If you get lost again, just find the rain tree and shout. Someone will hear you.' Then he was gone into the noise of the canteen. It was a tiny thing, only a few sentences. Yet it was the first time all morning that someone had spoken to me as though I belonged.

Paragraph 8
That evening my mother asked how my first day had been. I almost said 'terrible', because of the gate, the maze and the locked laboratory. Instead I thought of the rain tree, the empty desk by the window, and a quiet voice saying 'Come, I'll show you.' 'It was alright,' I told her, and I was surprised to find that I meant it. A new school is a big and frightening place. But it only takes one person to turn a stranger into a classmate.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1128 §B style practice, simplified-band difficulty; not from a past exam. Ipoh: a city in Malaysia; Secondary Three: the third year of secondary school in Singapore; recess: the mid-morning break; rain tree: a large, wide-spreading shade tree common in Singapore school grounds.)

questions.stem: Q1. In Paragraph 1, what details show that the narrator felt out of place at her new school? [1]
questions.answer: she gripped the strap of her new bag with both hands; the school was much bigger than her old one; the other students called out names and jokes she did not understand; and nobody looked at her — she felt like a small grey stone dropped into a fast-moving river

questions.stem: Q2. In Paragraph 2, why did the narrator become anxious even though her timetable 'sounded simple enough'? [1]
questions.answer: because the school was a maze of staircases and covered walkways where every corridor looked the same, so she got lost and ended up at a locked science laboratory; then the bell rang, meaning she was already late and did not know which way to turn

questions.stem: Q3. What is the effect of the image 'a small grey stone dropped into a fast-moving river' (Paragraph 1)? [1]
questions.answer: it vividly conveys how small, still and powerless the narrator felt among the rushing crowd of students — like a tiny stone swept along by a river, she is insignificant and unable to control or join the busy life moving around her

questions.stem: Q4. In Paragraph 3, what does the narrator mean when she says 'Here my voice seemed to have shrunk to nothing'? [1]
questions.answer: she means that although she used to be one of the loudest, most confident girls back home, in this strange new place she had become so nervous and unsure of herself that she could not even bring herself to speak or ask for help

questions.stem: Q5. In Paragraph 4, how do we know that the boy was kind rather than mocking? [1]
questions.answer: even though he had seen everything — her wrong turns, her panic and her shining eyes — he did not laugh; instead he quietly checked her timetable and offered to show her the way to Block C, treating her trouble gently rather than making fun of it

questions.stem: Q6. Why is the boy's comment 'Everyone gets lost until they learn the rain tree' (Paragraph 5) reassuring to the narrator? [1]
questions.answer: it tells her that getting lost is normal and that every new student goes through the same thing, so she is not stupid or alone; this makes her feel less embarrassed and more at ease, which is why she manages her first laugh of the morning

questions.stem: Q7. What is the effect of the closing sentence 'But it only takes one person to turn a stranger into a classmate' (Paragraph 8)? [1]
questions.answer: it sums up the whole lesson of the story — that a single small act of kindness can completely change how a frightened newcomer feels; it shows the narrator's relief and gratitude, and rounds off her shift from feeling alone to feeling that she now belongs

sections.instruction: Q8. The narrator's main feeling shifts across her first morning at the new school. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best describes her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: lonely / frightened / grateful / settled / cheerful / jealous / impatient / sleepy. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Newcomer' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift across her first morning — from lonely in Paragraph 1 when she stands alone at the gate while unfamiliar students stream past and nobody looks at her, to frightened in Paragraphs 2 and 3 when she gets lost in the maze of corridors, the bell rings and she cannot even make herself speak, to grateful in Paragraphs 4 and 5 when the boy quietly offers to show her the way and points out the landmarks, ending settled in Paragraph 6 when she sits at the empty desk by the window and the tight knot in her chest begins to loosen. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q8(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 1 as she stands alone at the school gate, gripping her bag while unfamiliar students stream past calling out jokes she does not understand and nobody looks at her? [1]

Q8(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 2 and 3 as she gets lost in the maze of staircases, reaches a locked laboratory just as the bell rings, and finds she cannot make herself speak to ask for help? [1]

Q8(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 4 and 5 when the boy, without laughing at her, quietly offers 'Come, I'll show you' and walks her to Block C, pointing out the rain tree and other landmarks along the way? [1]

Q8(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 6 once she reaches the classroom in time, sits at the empty desk by the window the boy points her to, and feels the tight knot in her chest begin to loosen? [1]
